ICONIC – ICON in the Cloud F. Prill, C. Eser
ICONIC – ICON in the Cloud
F. Prill, C. Eser
Deutscher Wetterdienst
Abstract
The commercial IT cloud market has evolved rapidly over the last decade. Running regional
numerical weather forecasts over the Internet, without the need for a local IT infrastructure,
no longer seems impossible. The German Weather Service (DWD) therefore develops a gen-
eral, portable toolchain around user-defined small and medium-size regional weather forecasts.
In particular, DWD’s ICONIC project has investigated the potential of this approach in a pre-
operational setup for Central Asia for the ICON-LAM numerical weather prediction model.
Resources have been funded 2021 – 2022 as a World Bank pilot, where this document repre-
sents a report of the project work.
Keywords: ICON, Cloud, Docker, Singularity, Scientific workloads, Mesoscale models,
Numerical weather prediction/forecasting
Correspondence to: F. Prill(florian.prill@dwd.de)
1. Introduction
The icosahedral non-hydrostatic model ICON has initially been developed by the German Weath-
er Service (DWD) and the Max-Planck-Institute for Meteorology (MPI-M) as a numerical weather
prediction (NWP) and climate modelling framework. In the wake of its successful introduction in
2015, other important MetServices and institutes have joined the effort, including MeteoSwiss and
the Swiss Supercomputing Centre. Within the Consortium for Small-scale Modeling (COSMO,
see [13]) the ICON model is now used operationally by numerous regional and national meteoro-
logical services.
Apart from these partnerships, the German Weather Service (DWD) has been supporting na-
tional hydrometeorological services worldwide for more than 20 years. DWD’s regional models
are free of charge for developing countries and universities. Various support services are targeted
at MetServices and scientific users who are planning to run DWD’s regional models. These sup-
porting activities range from training events to “classical” support channels, e.g. mailing lists and
F.A.Q. web sites, to site assistance in individual cases.
However, the complexity of the ICON model and the heterogeneity of today’s computer plat-
forms give rise to an increasing need for technical support: Users require advice on the model
setup and support during the installation phase on local computer systems. Parallel processing
is applied on a massive scale, where workloads change in the short term and the dynamic require-
ments exceed the available local hardware. This increasingly complex setup and software stack
Reports on ICON- Issue009 3
ICONIC – ICON in the Cloud F. Prill, C. Eser
is contrasted with the limited staff resources of small National Meteorological and Hydrological
Services (NMHSs) or university groups. Even large developer communities, such as that of the
ICON model, do not have the necessary support capacity for this situation. Within the ICONIC
project two key answers to these problems have been identified: container deployment and cloud
service models.
1.1. Containerization
During the last decade the container technology has emerged as the natural extension of classical
program binaries and libraries. Containers are based on so-called images, pre-configured ready for
use. An image is complete in itself: There is no need to consider software dependencies or library
versions, and containers can be used and reproduced almost anywhere. This includes remote
platforms as well as running on-premise with very broad limits of portability (e.g. architecture
changes, outdated kernels). Furthermore, containers are not virtual machines, but processes
isolated by kernel functions that run on the system like normal software. This means that there
is practically no loss of performance.
The first attempts with a containerized ICON model have shown the feasability of the approach
for numerical weather prediction. The complete model environment is containerized and can be
re-used without change on multiple machine types. Several pre- and postprocessing tools have
be

catalog_entry.summary: This paper discusses the ICONIC project which explores running regional numerical weather forecasts using cloud computing resources, specifically focusing on Central Asia with the ICON-LAM model.
